text.tspan: The main journey stays quiet; detail appears only on request in a closable overlay.
Why do cities preserve some memories—and erase others?
A concise answer from four checked sources.
Public memory is designed, not merely stored.
Cities make some histories durable through names, landmarks, archives, and rituals.
Other histories become harder to see through demolition, renaming, and neglect. [1–4]
A city's memory is a negotiation over what remains visible.
Library of Congress · Geography and Map Division
Who decides which memories become official?
Can a city recover a memory it erased?
IN-PLACE BUFFERING · NOT A SEPARATE PAGE

Why do cities preserve some memories—and erase others?
A concise answer from four checked sources.
Public memory is designed, not merely stored.
Cities make some histories durable through names, landmarks, archives, and rituals.
Other histories become harder to see through demolition, renaming, and neglect. [1–4]
A city's memory is a negotiation over what remains visible.
Library of Congress · Geography and Map Division
Who decides which memories become official?
Can a city recover a memory it erased?
How a city makes memory durable
Supporting detail without leaving the journey.
Names, monuments, transit maps, and preserved buildings repeatedly place
selected histories in public view. Repetition helps them feel settled and official. [1][2]
Demolition and renaming can weaken visible continuity. Communities often answer
with oral histories, family archives, protest, and informal place-names. [3][4]
Official memory is not the same as shared memory; the second can survive outside institutions.
[1] Library of Congress map collection [2] Chicago municipal archives
[3] National Park Service preservation guidance [4] Community oral-history collection

Research never opens an intermediate steps page: the mounted answer card buffers in place until content replaces its skeletons. Deeper dive opens above the stable journey and closes with ×, Escape, or outside click.
